other_observers: Andy Boyce; we found the birds
specific_location: A hundred yards west of where US 50 bends east on the north side of the Arkansas River north of Las Animas, on the north side of the county road.
description: There were ten ibis present: two GLIBs and eight WFIBs. The WFIBs were in various plumages--one with a good half-inch of white feathering all around the facial skin; another two without any trace of white feathering; the rest in between. All the WFIBs had red eyes and facial skin that was varying degrees of red. Most had entirely red legs, though there were some with red mostly around the "kneecaps." The GLIBs, in contrast, both had dark (brown) eyes, and neither had any trace of white feathering anywhere about the head (or elsewhere, for that matter). The edge of the facial skin was electric blue (we also used the words "sky blue" and "robin's-egg blue" to describe it) in a thin line of uniform thickness going from the bottom edge of the mandible up to the bottom of the eye, and then from the top of the eye across the forehead to the other eye. Thus, this pale blue border was discontinuous at the eye. The rest of the facial skin, inward of these lines and out to a vertical border with the base of the bill, was a much softer, darker bluish color. It probably would have looked dark had the light not been good or had we been farther away, but our excellent views allowed us to see that it had a bluish cast. The legs on the GLIBs were a pale grayish horn color, about the same color as the bill. One GLIB had a faint reddish wash to the anterior portions of the "knees." This would not have been visible at any greater distance or in poorer light. This same GLIB had ever-so-slightly thinner blue borders to the facial skin, but in all other respects (eye color, facial skin color and pattern) it was identical to the other GLIB.Behaviors: All ten ibis were foraging actively in a wet field in the company of blackbirds and a few ducks. Sometimes they would stop to preen or to stretch their wings above their heads.Call: birds in the flock were grunting, like hoarse Mallards, but it was impossible to tell whether these sounds were coming from the WFIBs, the GLIBs or both.Plumage: adult alternate
